speaker: interviewer
text: --with Rachel Huber. It's the 28th of May, 1996. Rachel, you mentioned that you yourself realized your luck wasn't going to continue. How did you persuade your father to do something else, and what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the way I persuaded him was by crying and-- and-- and and screaming, I want to live. That seemed to have been the-- the slogan then. There were not many avenues open. But my father had a friend who was how do you-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a projectionist-- that's what you say? Yeah. And he was-- as if he would manage the whole cinema.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not too many cinemas in Cernauti at that time. I don't know if there were-- three or four cinemas, most probably. And that was the biggest cinema. And he was from the first that got an authorization that they needed him. So he was secure in his job that they would need him and he-- he wouldn't be taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only remember his first name. And it was Salom. And my father had a good to talk to him. And he agreed-- there was no remuneration in that involved at all-- he was a Jewish man-- to hide us in-- in the cinema, in a very-- in a small room, a dark room. It was all concrete. It was very cold there. In time, my feet started-- I still have got, until today, frostbite from that. Whenever it's cold, it starts to flare up. It was cold. But there was no choice. There was a tiny little window there-- it was more a hole than a window-- from which I could see how the-- pardon me-- how the Germans proceeded in the next deportations. I saw them tearing the beards of the old Jews. I saw them beating-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, these were not Germans. These were Romanians. I'm sorry. The Germans-- it-- those were not Germans. That were Romanians that did that. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I forgot to tell you before was that at the beginning-- I'm sorry, if I can jump now--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Keep going. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing that was done in Cernauti, basically, was the burning of the temple. We had a beautiful, beautiful temple. And on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, when-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1941. In 1941. That would be-- we had-- 22 of June was the outbreak of the war. Eight days later, we had German occupation. That must have been a month later or six weeks later. In-- it must have been, maybe, in August, 1941. The first thing they did, they burned the temple. And they burned the chief rabbi, Rabbi Marks. They burned him. They asked him to come and to open the-- the-- the temple. And he was inside and I do not know what-- how they burned it. But I remember it was a Friday night and we saw the-- the-- the sky became red. And we heard the-- the crackling. We lived not very far. And that was the first thing they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- Rachel, did they burn the rabbi in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. That was the first omen, the first signal, that we are in for-- for death. We were in hiding--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go into hiding? When did you go to the cinema?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, the years are a little bit-- memory is not accurate. I think it was '42, '43. I think it was and of '42, maybe-- something. Because I remember the cold. So I must have been a winter there. I think it was '42, '43. In '43, they stopped the deportations. Most probably because they were already in retreat from Stalingrad. Most probably that. But we were never too sure. We were never too sure what the next wave might bring. So we had to be in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we were already liberated in March, I think, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, let's go back to the hiding in the cinema.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the cinema a very big building?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Compared to Sydney, certainly, it was a small cinema. But for Cernauti, it was a big building. There was-- as I said before, that friend of my father's, he managed, somehow, the whole cinema. And there wasn't-- there were not many people-- the-- he had-- didn't have such a big staff. He would be the projectionist. He didn't have anybody to-- to help him. So he would-- he was somehow in control of that enterprise. And we did not have any fear for somebody coming in during the day, or somebody looking for something. Because he locked it. He locked it. So nobody would have come. He had the keys. When the cinema was over, when the picture were over, 11 o'clock at night, we could get out from ours-- there were nobody in the cinema-- then he would open this. And in the morning, he would come and would lock it again. So we could move a little bit around, then, in the cinema. Whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he get food for you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] Don't really know where he get it-- he bought it. He bought it where-- where would it g-- mind you, that there was not so much supply that could be oversupply. I remember lying there and remembering every crumb that I ever left, every piece of cake that my mother asked me to eat and I didn't eat. I remembered all the little things that I left behind, or that I let-- in school that I threw out. And I remember then I thought if I wouldn't that have now. But-- but we-- we didn't starve. We had enough bread. We didn't starve, which was the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just lie there. Cannot-- I cannot recollect any particular thing. Look out from the window. Look out for my father he shouldn't be sick again. Pep up my mother that she should not lose her-- she was very pessimistic from nature. And she's just thought that we are lost anyhow, so what for do we-- do we have to go through all that. So to keep my parents going was a full-time job. I don't remember what I did. I just remember that I talked a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything to read? Did you play cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dark there. It was dark there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't make-- I don't even remember how it was light-- if we had light there, electric light. I can't remember that. We had the-- that-- that-- that hole from that little hole that was how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Complicated problem. Whatever was in the pail was, at night, disposed of.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned being able to look out of the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a little hole. It was not a window, a really big window. It was a-- very small. I could peep out. It was a ray of sun somehow. And I could see what's going on on the place around the cinema, a little bit. A little bit. It was some sort of like-- like I have contact with the outside world. Not just a hole in the-- in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: The window of-- or the little gap--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you looked through, what did that face?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an outer wall. That were the outer wall. It faced a little place, not built, not built. That was like a-- like Martin Place, for instance. It was a rotunda. It was a round place with a few trees. And around that were built houses and among them was the cinema. When I looked out, I could see the street. I could see those-- those few trees. And I could see, vaguely, the people that were walking around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you saw a subsequent action, deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. All I saw, all I saw, basically from that, was a religious Jew tearing his-- tearing him at the beard through the street. People running. People screaming. Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen on more than one occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can remember only one occasion. I only remember one occasion. Yes. I don't remember any other occasions there. There was one occasion. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whilst you were hiding in the little room, you were never disturbed by staff or people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --working in the cinema.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very lucky. That was not-- that was the spot-- I do not know how to can describe it. That was not something that was used. It was an unused part. People did not-- the people that worked in the cinema did not have, somehow, access there. They did not walk around there. We-- we didn't have, even once, fright. We didn't have any fear whatsoever there. Because it never happened. We had fear that something might happen. But nothing actually did happen to us there. We walked out from there when we knew that-- that the-- the German army is retreating. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was a unwell man. Could you get medications for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Salom would bring him, he would bring him-- he-- all he needed was those nitroglycerins for under the tongue. Yes. He would bring him that. We were very lucky, really, that we had somebody who should look after us and should be a human being and helping. Because basically, he endangered himself. If we would be found out, he would have gone just the same. They would have taken him too, because he would-- enabled us to be there. So he really endangered himself. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he, this gentleman, Salom, who-- who looked after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did he have a family, wife, children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a-- he had a wife, but he had no children. He had no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did his--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know until today if his wife knew or not. I don't know that. I don't know. Somehow, when one looks back, so many-- so many gaps in memory are-- are somehow evolving. I don't know even if I knew at the time if she know-- knew or not. I just don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What news did you have of the progress of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I tell you. We had a tiny, tiny little radio in that room, that he brought us. And in that, tiny, tiny, very softly, we heard BBC from England. And that kept us really going, that the front is coming nearer and nearer, and that the time of our liberation comes nearer. We had the-- the BBC. That sound-- I-- I will remember my whole life. The sound from the BBC coming on. And that was-- that was a ray of hope that kept us alive. Those news that-- that they're coming nearer. And I remember, for some reason, the Russians decided-- before they came to Cernauti-- decided to attack on another front. And for two or three months, the-- the borders remained far from us. And we were so desperate-- they should already come. Every day was-- was a-- a question mark. So, oh, we lived in hope. We lived in hope. And I particular, was such a young, enthusiastic person, my hope somehow was hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought, I will die. I never thought. I had fear. I knew that I have to-- to fight for each and every moment. But I never envisaged that I would die. I think that's very negative thinking. All I-- all I tried was to occupy myself and keep my parents going, that they should not collapse emotionally. But I somehow didn't think about dying. No. I don't think even today, even that I'm 70, 70 and 1/2, and today, I do not think about myself dying. I know that the time is going to come. And I know that I'm old and I have got all sorts of sicknesses. But I don't think of death. I think that one should concentrate on life. Whatever comes, comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide that you were safe enough to leave your hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German retreat. The Germans were in retreat. And all the people that were in hiding-- there were a few people-- quite a few people-- everybody-- because that-- there was-- the-- the Romanians were-- were packing. They knew that their days are limited. And then the German military retreated. We knew that that is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to have a look at them retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw them, which was very-- what's the-- the right word? It was not very prudent to go and look at them, even from a window, it was not very prudent. And I-- we should have stayed put in hiding. But again, there was me with my exuberance. Because there was a plan by the Germans that the ghettos should be put alight, should be burned, and with it, all the people inside the ghetto. And just why the Romanians didn't lit it, we-- I don't know until today. It could be that it involved, again, somebody in Bucharest intervening with money or somewhat. I do not know. I know only for sure that the American joined-- did a lot of help, monetary help. And that was all channeled to Bucharest, in order to bribe the high officials in as much as helping that the whole Jewish problem should somehow be contained as much as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you talk about all of this, were you still in your room in the cinema?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the German--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw-- no. When-- when I saw them, that they-- they are retreating, I wasn't anymore in the cinema. No. No. I was not. I remember a window.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave the cinema?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. During that upheaval. During that upheaval. I can't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to leave? Why did you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Salom came and said, the-- the-- the Germans are leaving. The Germans are leaving. The Romanians are leaving. There was upheaval. That was a whole upheaval.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the Romanians were leaving--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanians were the administrative part.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fascist Romanians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the army was the German army that went through on the retreat, that they came from the frontiers they came, all motorized. All with their-- with their gloves. And with their-- you wouldn't have thought for one moment that they're retreating. But they were retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did see them retreating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did. And I was-- [LAUGHS] I was exhilarated to see them go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you watched them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a window. But don't ask me where that window was. Because I don't know today where that was. I remember only that I was at the window and saw them-- saw them retreating. Behind a curtain, I looked out, not in full flight. Because you are so fearful for everything. But I saw them retreating. That was the most-- one of the most beautiful sights that can be imagined.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time they-- you witnessed the retreat, you had actually left the cinema.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yep. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to our original home. The way it looked, after all that, there were-- that was-- the whole house of my grandfather was so-called nationalized, or taking on the administration. And they had planks put over the main entrance that nobody could come in. Most probably, they wanted afterwards, whatever to do. I do not know. Anyhow, we went back. And we took off all the planks. And we went into it. And my mother started to cry that she was back home, that she was alive. And-- and there it is. And then came the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, 1944. February or March, somehow. Somehow, I-- I-- I'm not so-- I-- after all those years, from '44 till '96, how many years did we have--

speaker: interviewer
text: 52 years? Or how many? 52 years since then or whatever. I-- I-- I am-- I'm not so sure about the-- the dates. But I think it was March-- it's definitely 1944. When were you liberated by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you go back to the ghetto before the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During that-- during that retreat. There were several days that they-- they retreated. The armies passed by.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Romanians left. They-- they they took all their families and all that. That was the whole atmosphere of retreat, you know? That one could have seen that-- that there is no danger anymore, more or less. When was this rumor happening that the Germans were going to surround the ghetto and burn it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not surround. They were to burn it. That rumor-- when was the rumor? I think-- I think that we found out that after the Germans left. I think we found that out. That was afterwards that we found out that there was a-- an order by the Germans that the Romanians did not obey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The Russians. Well, the unit that liberated us was a unit from Middle East-- Middle Asia. They were [NON-ENGLISH], they were called. Very tiny, tiny soldiers. They were lying on the-- afterwards they were lying in the-- in the town hall of the place, and sleeping on the floor, dirty and rumpled. And we thought, what a wonderful miracle of God is that, that those soldiers defeated those organized Germans [LAUGHS] that were so-- so organized, they were sitting on those motorbikes, that you could think that-- that they are really [INAUDIBLE]. And they were defeated. At that time, we thought it must be a sign of God, that God was on the right side this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated well by the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They saw-- they were on advance from middle Russia. They went through the whole of the Ukraine. And the whole of Ukraine was burnt out and devastated by the Germans in their retreat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they arrived in Cernauti at Cernauti, we still had houses standing and there was still, so to speak, a city that was not destroyed, it was a miracle for them. It was a miracle. They were very nice to us, in their way, very detached. But nice to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were preoccupied with their war, with their problems. They liberated us, they went further, and then came the Russian administration. And then that started the normal life being organized. One of the first things they did-- pardon me-- were taking whoever young people were-- were not that many-- who were young people to enlisting them in the army. Even my father was a-- was taken to the red army. [LAUGHS] but-- they tried to take him to the army. But he could weed himself out. He was at that time, most probably, already 50 or 51. But, being a tailor, he made a suit for-- for the one that was in the commission and then the other one, and so on. And he stayed with us. He didn't have to go anywhere. That was the first. Afterwards, a few months later, I started to go back to school. Because I-- I wanted to finish my education. But that was-- in Russia, there were only 10 years of schooling. And I was supposed to finish the 10-- the year 10. But after half a year, maybe, they closed the schools. And they said, it must be-- the effort for the war must be intensified. And the women were supposed to go and work in the coal-- coal mines in-- they were called, in Russian, Donbass. This is Donetskij bassejn, that is in the middle of Russia. Well, that-- it was another hurdle to be overcome. Very hard to overcome until, through my father's friend, again, I got a job learning to be a projectionist in a cinema again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this man who had saved your family now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. He made it through his-- his acquaintances, you know, one man, one person knows this-- the second one. And I started to work as a projectionist. And that saved me from having being sent away to Donbass.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were under the Russians-- a year later was the end of the war, on the 8th of May. With the ending of the war, a new chapter started which was called repatriation.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Repatriation of all those people that were dislocated, were sent away, by the Germans, or taken away, in different parts of Europe. They dislocated the whole of-- of Europe, where Jews are concerned or what the other minorities that they-- one, for instance, gypsies-- that they dislocated. And what-- what affected us was that my father being-- having-- having been a Polish citizen, and I still had that passport that were-- we were under Swiss protectorate, we could have-- we could go back to Poland as-- with the repatriation. That meant that we would escape Russia. That meant for the last time of finishing of the chapter Cernauti. Leaving-- leaving again, with a rucksack, in peacetime so-- so to speak, after the war, but leaving Cernauti. Because it was under Russian. And under no circumstances did we want to live under communist rule.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Cernauti?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- in August, 1945. That were a few months after-- we went-- after end of the war. We went with a cattle wagon through Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one of the most memorable things that happened to me was we were sitting in the wagon and passing by-- that I assume it was Auschwitz. But I assume. I'm not so sure. When we saw peasants digging, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and bones and craniums? Yes? Skulls. Skulls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skulls. Skulls. And skulls were just flying, were coming out. And you-- you saw them sitting-- there were masses of peasants, digging out. And they were just flying, all those-- those bones and those cranium. And all I felt were, who knows, who knows who they are. And most probably, they are Jews. I think that was for the first time that I had-- that I really came to see the-- the reality that what the Germans really did. Because all the rumors that we heard before-- that the Germans are killing, and the Germans are gassing-- we-- we thought these were only rumors. It is-- it is-- couldn't be that way. We refused to believe it. And that was the first time that I really saw those and I associated with what we heard. And I thought that might be some truth in it. We passed a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, let's stop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
